lHmMK_4AAAAJ.keywords: Computer Security, Cryptography, Policy, Vulnerability Discovery, Explainable AI, Election Security, Voter Registration Security, Privacy-preserving Systems, Mobile and IoT Security, Cryptographic Vulnerabilities
lHmMK_4AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Michael A. Specter is a principal researcher and faculty member at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group focused on applied **computer security** and **cryptography** with strong ties to public-interest **policy** work. His work combines *systems-oriented vulnerability discovery* and *empirical measurement* with policy-minded analysis to improve the security and trustworthiness of deployed systems. Across his portfolio he emphasizes <u>voting security</u>, <u>cryptographic vulnerabilities</u>, and <u>privacy-preserving system design</u>, bringing technical rigor to problems that have direct societal impact.

---

## Research Areas
Specter’s research spans the intersection of engineering, measurement, and policy. He has produced high-impact work on the interpretability of machine learning (e.g., "Explaining Explanations: An Overview of Interpretability of Machine Learning"), showing an interest in how complex algorithmic systems can be made transparent and accountable. In the domain of cryptography and system security he studies real-world weaknesses in libraries and protocols ("You really shouldn't roll your own crypto", "Cryptography in the wild"), the economics and trust models of certificate authorities ("The economics of cryptographic trust"), and pragmatic mitigations for email and messaging compromises ("KeyForge: Mitigating Email Breaches with Forward-Forgeable Signatures"). A recurring theme in his work is election security: he has produced system-level audits and threat analyses of internet voting platforms ("The Ballot is Busted Before the Blockchain", "Security Analysis of the Democracy Live Online Voting System") and broader critiques of proposals to move voting onto novel platforms ("Going from Bad to Worse: From Internet Voting to Blockchain Voting"). Methodologically, Specter uses empirical measurement, security analysis, protocol specification, and systematization-of-knowledge approaches to reveal vulnerabilities and recommend actionable mitigations, often coupling technical findings with policy recommendations (e.g., "Keys under doormats").

---

## Notable Works
- "Explaining Explanations: An Overview of Interpretability of Machine Learning" (2018) — A widely cited synthesis of explainable AI research that frames technical approaches to interpretability and their limitations, helping shape subsequent work on trustworthy ML.
- "Keys under doormats" (2015) — A policy-technical analysis arguing against mandatory government access mechanisms; the paper connects cryptographic design, deployability, and the broad security harms of backdoors.
- "The Ballot is Busted Before the Blockchain: A Security Analysis of Voatz, the First Internet Voting Application Used in US Federal Elections" (2020) — A pointed, system-level security audit of a deployed mobile voting app that exposed significant risks to election integrity and informed public and policy debates.

---

## Academic Background
Michael A. Specter received his Ph.D. in **EECS from MIT** and is currently affiliated with **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a security-focused research group and mentors students and collaborators across security, privacy, and policy. His publications have accumulated over **5,111** citations, reflecting influence both in academic venues and in public-facing policy discussions. Specter’s body of work bridges theory and practice: he produces peer-reviewed empirical studies, protocol specifications, and security audits that target deployed systems of societal importance (elections, mobile platforms, contact tracing, and web cryptography). For more information, papers, and group information, see his homepage: http://mikespecter.com/.

q1bkP7AAAAAJ.keywords: modeling and simulation, parallel computing, distributed computing, distributed simulation, parallel discrete event simulation, optimistic synchronization (Time Warp), reverse computation, High Level Architecture (HLA), network simulation, vehicular networks (V2V/VANET)
q1bkP7AAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Richard Fujimoto is a **Regents' Professor** at the Georgia Institute of Technology in the College of Computing, where he leads a long-established research effort in *modeling and simulation*. His group focuses on the design, analysis, and implementation of scalable simulation systems for complex engineered and networked systems. Fujimoto's work is centrally concerned with <u>parallel and distributed simulation</u> techniques — in particular, methods for synchronization and time management such as <u>parallel discrete event simulation (PDES)</u> and the <u>Time Warp</u> optimistic synchronization paradigm — as well as applications of these techniques to large-scale *network simulation* and *vehicular communications*.

---

## Research Areas
Fujimoto's research spans the theory, algorithms, and systems engineering of scalable simulation. He has contributed foundational treatment of **parallel discrete event simulation**, exploring both conservative and optimistic synchronization strategies (see "Parallel discrete event simulation" (1990) and "Optimistic approaches to parallel discrete event simulation" (1990)). He has advanced practical mechanisms for optimistic simulation, notably through work on <u>Time Warp</u>, global virtual time computation, and rollback techniques including *reverse computation* ("Efficient optimistic parallel simulations using reverse computation" (1999)). Fujimoto has also shaped standards and infrastructure for distributed simulation: his papers on the *High Level Architecture* and time management ("The department of defense high level architecture" (1997); "Time management in the high level architecture" (1998)) address interoperability and temporal correctness in federated simulations. On the applications side, he has researched scalable packet-level network simulation and federated approaches to partitioning network models ("Large-scale network simulation: how big? how fast?" (2003); "A federated approach to distributed network simulation" (2004)), and developed analytic and algorithmic treatments of information propagation and dissemination in vehicular networks ("MDDV: a mobility-centric data dissemination algorithm for vehicular networks" (2004); "Analytical models for information propagation in vehicle-to-vehicle networks" (2004)). Across these domains Fujimoto emphasizes measurable performance, implementation trade-offs (e.g., RTI implementations, rollback hardware proposals), and methods for making high-fidelity simulation practical at scale.

---

## Notable Works
- "Parallel and Distributed Simulation Systems" (2000) — a comprehensive synthesis of the field that surveys algorithms, architectures, and application areas and has served as a reference point for both researchers and practitioners in large-scale simulation.
- "Parallel discrete event simulation" (1990) — a foundational tutorial and research contribution that helped define the problems and solution approaches for executing discrete event simulations on parallel and distributed platforms.
- "Efficient optimistic parallel simulations using reverse computation" (1999) — a seminal paper introducing reverse computation as an efficient alternative to state saving for rollback in optimistic PDES, enabling more efficient optimistic execution in many simulation settings.

---

## Academic Background
Fujimoto has been an influential figure in modeling and simulation since the field's formative decades (1970s–1990s) and holds the title of **Regents' Professor** at the Georgia Institute of Technology, reflecting a sustained record of scholarly impact and service. His publication record (several hundred papers and textbooks/tutorials) and high citation count (approximately 19,847 citations) document widespread influence across both theory and systems. He has engaged in community leadership through edited volumes and workshops (for example, the Dagstuhl report and NSF-sponsored workshops on modeling and simulation), and his work has intersected with defense and standards communities through contributions to the *High Level Architecture* and related runtime infrastructure design. Fujimoto's career combines deep theoretical contributions (synchronization theory, time management, analytic performance models) with practical system-building (RTI implementations, experiments on shared-memory and distributed platforms), mentoring of students and collaborators at Georgia Tech, and participation in multidisciplinary efforts to scale simulation for complex engineered systems.

q_K8iFQAAAAJ.keywords: Cognitive Systems, Artificial Intelligence in Education, Human-AI Teaming, Concept Formation, Intelligent Tutoring Systems, Learner Modeling, Tutor Authoring, Incremental Learning, Probabilistic Concept Formation, Educational Data Mining
q_K8iFQAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Christopher J. MacLellan is an **Assistant Professor** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group working at the intersection of *cognitive systems* and *artificial intelligence in education*. His lab develops computational models and interactive systems that support <u>human-AI teaming</u>, the automated construction of <u>intelligent tutoring systems (ITS)</u>, and mechanisms for incremental <u>concept formation</u>. Across empirical, computational, and applied projects, MacLellan's work emphasizes building *interpretable, theory-driven* agents and authoring tools that close the loop between learning theory, learner modeling, and classroom- or learner-facing AI.

---

## Research Areas
MacLellan’s research spans cognitive modeling, learning sciences, and applied AI. He has advanced methods for automated tutor authoring (e.g., SimStudent and the Apprentice Learner framework) that reduce the cost of building ITSs and enable *educator-driven* personalization; explored algorithms for incremental and probabilistic concept formation (TRESTLE, Cobweb-derived approaches, and Cobweb4V) for structured and visual domains; and investigated how machine agents can be designed to function as effective members of human-machine teams (team science perspectives on teamwork competencies). His publications also cover evaluation methods for open-ended educational games (replay analysis, feature extraction), modeling slips and false negatives in student performance data, knowledge tracing in online mastery contexts, and recent work integrating large language models (LLMs) and GPT-based dialog parsing into interactive task learning and feedback generation for tutors.

---

## Notable Works
- "Improving teamwork competencies in human-machine teams: Perspectives from team science" (2021) — a perspective piece that synthesizes team science to guide the design of machine agents that can support and augment human team performance.
- "Domain-general tutor authoring with apprentice learner models" (2022) — a project that demonstrates how apprentice learner architectures can enable domain-general, scalable tutor authoring to broaden access to ITS technology.
- "The Apprentice Learner Architecture: Closing the loop between learning theory and educational data" (2016) — a foundational paper describing an architecture that ties computational learning theory to educational data, enabling simulated learners to inform tutor design and evaluation.

---

## Academic Background
Christopher J. MacLellan’s scholarship blends cognitive science, computer science, and learning sciences. He has produced a sustained body of work over more than a decade on computational models of learning, tutor authoring, and concept formation, and his publications have attracted substantial citations across educational data mining and AI in education communities (total citations ~980). As an Assistant Professor at Georgia Tech, he contributes to interdisciplinary training and collaborates with educators, cognitive scientists, and AI researchers. His research has been disseminated through both methodological contributions (models and algorithms) and applied systems (authoring platforms, tutor interfaces, LLM-based feedback tools), and has been recognized through competitive academic channels and external research support. For more information and a current list of projects, see his homepage: https://chrismaclellan.com/.

vlV_GoEAAAAJ.keywords: Space, AI, Security, Edge Cloud, Distributed Systems, IoT DDoS mitigation, LEO satellite networks, Edge orchestration, Edge machine learning, Lightweight containers for edge
vlV_GoEAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Ketan Bhardwaj is a faculty researcher in the College of Computing at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads and contributes to a systems-focused research agenda within the broader *cloud, edge and distributed systems* community. His work centers on designing practical, secure and scalable infrastructure for next-generation computing at the network edge — with particular emphasis on <u>edge-cloud architectures</u>, <u>security for edge and function offload</u>, and the emerging <u>Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite compute tier</u>. He unites systems design, networking, and applied machine learning to tackle real-world problems such as **IoT-driven DDoS mitigation**, **fast secure onloading of edge functions**, and **efficient ML model serving at constrained edge sites**.

---

## Research Areas
Bhardwaj’s research spans several tightly connected areas in systems and networking. He develops architectures and mechanisms for *edge computing* (e.g., “Fast, scalable and secure onloading of edge functions using AirBox” and “Ecc: Edge cloud composites”), focusing on rapid, secure provisioning of edge functions and low-latency delivery of services (“Creation and delivery of pre-rendered web pages for accelerated browsing”, “AppSachet”, “AppFlux”). Security and resilience at the edge are recurring themes: his work on IoT-driven application-layer DDoS mitigation (“Towards IoT‑DDoS prevention using edge computing”) and preserving end-to-end security for multi-tenant edge deployments (“SPX: Preserving end-to-end security for edge computing”) address how to retain trust and robustness when computation moves closer to users. He also advances ML inference and model-slicing techniques for resource-constrained sites (“Couper: DNN model slicing for visual analytics containers at the edge”, “Pocket: ML serving from the edge”), and investigates the unique challenges of using LEO satellites as an edge tier — orchestration, routing variability, and scheduling for a LEO compute cloud (“Toward Loosely Coupled Orchestration for the LEO Satellite Edge”, “A characterization of route variability in LEO satellite networks”, “Krios: Scheduling abstractions and mechanisms for enabling a LEO compute cloud”). Across these topics he addresses resource heterogeneity, low-latency SLAs, multi-tenancy, and the interplay between infrastructure placement and application-level performance.

---

## Notable Works
- "Towards IoT‑DDoS prevention using edge computing" (2018) — Proposes an edge-driven approach to detect and mitigate application-level, seemingly-legitimate DDoS traffic generated by compromised IoT devices, demonstrating how localized edge defenses can overcome scale and distribution challenges of IoT botnets.  
- "Fast, scalable and secure onloading of edge functions using AirBox" (2016) — Introduces AirBox, a back-end driven mechanism for rapid, scalable, and security-preserving onloading of edge functions, addressing bandwidth, latency, and trust requirements for device–cloud interactions at the edge.  
- "Couper: DNN model slicing for visual analytics containers at the edge" (2019) — Presents model-slicing techniques and container abstractions to decouple DNN resource demands from edge node constraints, enabling latency-sensitive visual analytics to run effectively on distributed edge infrastructure.

---

## Academic Background
Ketan Bhardwaj has been an active researcher in edge and distributed systems since at least 2014, contributing to a body of work that crosses mobile systems, content delivery, security, and satellite-enabled edge computing. Affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology** and visible through his College of Computing homepage, he has published extensively in top systems and networking venues and accumulated over **766 citations**, reflecting broad influence across the edge/IoT/security communities. His publication record shows sustained collaborations on problems such as app delivery, ML serving at the edge, and LEO satellite orchestration, and his projects bridge academic research and practical system-building — attracting attention from both research peers and infrastructure practitioners. He regularly mentors students and collaborates across disciplines to translate systems research into deployable edge-cloud solutions.

zp8V7ZMAAAAJ.keywords: Robotic Manipulation, Robot Learning, Reinforcement Learning, Machine Learning, Computer Vision, Multimodal Representation Learning, Imitation Learning, Simulation and Sim-to-Real Transfer, Language-guided Task Planning, Offline Reinforcement Learning
zp8V7ZMAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Animesh Garg is a faculty researcher with joint affiliation at the **Georgia Institute of Technology** and the **University of Toronto**, where he leads a research group that sits at the intersection of *robotics*, *machine learning*, and *computer vision* (see http://animesh.garg.tech/). His work centers on developing data-driven methods for autonomous manipulation and embodied intelligence, with particular emphasis on <u>robotic manipulation</u>, <u>robot learning</u>, <u>multimodal perception</u>, and the use of <u>large language models</u> to bridge high-level instruction and low-level robot control. He combines large-scale datasets, simulation frameworks, and algorithmic advances in reinforcement and imitation learning to enable robots to plan, perceive, and act robustly in contact-rich, unstructured environments.

---

## Research Areas
Garg’s research spans several tightly connected areas within robotics and machine learning. He studies scalable approaches to <u>robot learning from data</u>, including dataset creation and model families that generalize across embodiments (e.g., Open X-Embodiment, 2023) and crowd-sourced imitation data collection (RoboTurk, 2018; Scaling RoboTurk, 2019). He advances multimodal representation learning for contact-rich tasks by fusing vision and touch (Making sense of vision and touch, 2019/2020). On control and policy learning, his work addresses action-space design (Variable Impedance Control, 2019), offline and safety-aware RL (Pessimistic Bootstrapping, 2022; Conservative Safety Critics, 2021; S4RL, 2021), and methods for learning from offline demonstrations at scale (IRIS, 2020). Garg also develops tools and simulation environments that accelerate research (ORBIT, 2023; DiSECt, 2021; Accelerated Policy Learning with Parallel Differentiable Simulation, 2022), and he explores bridging natural language and robotics, using large language models for task planning and domain-specific automation such as chemistry robotics and situated task planning (Progprompt, 2023; Large language models for chemistry robotics, 2023). Across these topics, he integrates perception (e.g., text-video retrieval, transparent object perception), causality and dynamics (Causal discovery in physical systems, 2020), and applications ranging from assistive arms to surgical automation.

---

## Notable Works
- "Progprompt: Generating situated robot task plans using large language models" (2023) — Demonstrated how *large language models* can be used to generate and score action sequences for robot task planning, reducing manual domain specification and enabling flexible high-level instruction grounding.  
- "Open X-Embodiment: Robotic learning datasets and RT-X models" (2023) — Released scalable datasets and model protocols enabling cross-embodiment generalization, a practical step toward training models that transfer across different robot bodies and sensors.  
- "Making sense of vision and touch: Self-supervised learning of multimodal representations for contact-rich tasks" (2019) — Advanced self-supervised multimodal representation learning that fuses haptic and visual signals to improve performance on contact-rich manipulation.

---

## Academic Background
Animesh Garg’s publication record shows a sustained trajectory from early work in surgical automation and trajectory segmentation to large-scale robot learning, simulation, and language-grounded planning. His body of work includes influential contributions to dataset creation (RoboTurk, Open X-Embodiment), simulation and benchmarking (ORBIT, DiSECt), algorithmic innovations in reinforcement and offline learning, and multimodal perception. He has accumulated over **15,151 citations**, reflecting broad community impact and adoption of his datasets, tools, and methods. Garg holds an academic appointment with both **Georgia Institute of Technology** and the **University of Toronto**, and his research group collaborates across robotics, ML, and vision centers to translate algorithmic advances into practical systems for manipulation, assistive robotics, and laboratory automation.
